title_en: The Next Thing You Smell Could Ruin Your Life
title_zh: 下一口气味可能毁掉你的人生

paragraphs.en: After my birth, my mother became allergic to the world. That’s the only way I knew how to put it. So many things could set her off: new carpeting, air fresheners, plastic off-gassing, diesel. Perfumes were among the worst offenders. On top of that, she developed terrible food allergies. The sound of her sniffling became the chorus of my childhood. Some days she couldn’t get out of bed. I’d peek into her darkened room and see her face pinched in discomfort.
paragraphs.zh: 出生以后，我妈妈就像是对整个世界产生了过敏。那是我能形容这种状况最贴切的说法。许多东西都会触发她：新装的地毯、空气清新剂、塑料挥发出来的气味、柴油味。香水更是最罪魁祸首之一。除此之外，她还出现了严重的食物过敏。她抽噎的声音成了我童年的背景音。有些日子她连床都下不去。我会悄悄探进她关着灯的房间，看见她的脸憋着，满是不适。

paragraphs.en: Her joints ached, her head swam. Doctors suggested that maybe she was depressed or anxious. “Well, you’d be anxious too if you couldn’t lick an envelope, couldn’t pick up your daughter in a car,” she’d reply. She tried allergists, got nowhere. Finally, she found her way to holistic health, whose practitioners told her she had something called multiple chemical sensitivity.
paragraphs.zh: 她的关节会痛，头也会发晕。医生建议她可能是抑郁或焦虑了。她会回道：“要是你连信封都咬不开、连孩子都不能坐到车里接，谁不会焦虑呢？”她尝试过看过敏专科医生，但都无果。直到最后，她走进了整体疗法体系，那里的从业者告诉她，她患的是一种叫“多重化学物质敏感症”的情况。

paragraphs.en: As long as people have complained that man-made stuff in their environment causes health problems—migraines and asthma, exhaustion and mood swings—the medical establishment has largely dismissed them. The American Medical Association, World Health Organization, and the American Academy of Asthma, Allergy & Immunology don’t recognize chemical sensitivity as a diagnosis. If they talk about it at all, they tend to dismiss it as psychosomatic, a malady of the neurotic and health-obsessed. Why, these authorities wondered, would people react to minute traces of a huge array of chemicals? And why couldn’t they ever seem to get better?
paragraphs.zh: 只要有人抱怨说环境中的人为物质会引发健康问题——偏头痛、哮喘、精疲力尽和情绪波动——医学主流长期以来基本都把这类声音当作无稽之谈。美国医学会、世界卫生组织和美国哮喘、过敏与免疫学会都不承认“化学敏感症”是一种诊断。即使他们提及，通常也会把它归因于心身性问题，认为这是神经质和过度关注健康的人的毛病。那些权威不解的是：为什么人会对各种各样化学物质的极微量痕迹产生反应？又为什么他们似乎始终得不到好转？

paragraphs.en: This isn’t some trivial affliction. Roughly a quarter of American adults report some form of chemical sensitivity; it lives alongside chronic pain and fibromyalgia as both evidently real and resistant to mainstream diagnosis or treatment. My mom tried a thousand things—elimination diets, antihistamines, lymphatic massage, antidepressants, acupuncture, red light therapy, saunas, heavy-metal detoxes. Sometimes her symptoms eased, but she never got better. Her illness ruled our lives, dictating what products we bought, what food we ate, where we traveled. I felt there had to be an answer for why this was happening. It didn’t take me long to learn that, if there was one, it’d come from a figure as unassuming as she is provocative: the scientist Claudia Miller.
paragraphs.zh: 这绝非小题大作。大约四分之一的美国成年人报告过某种形式的化学敏感，它与慢性疼痛和纤维肌痛一样，既真实存在，又顽固地不被主流诊断和治疗体系接受。我妈妈试过成千上万种方法——排除饮食、抗组胺药、淋巴按摩、抗抑郁药、针灸、红光疗法、桑拿、重金属排毒。她有时症状会缓解，却从未真正好转。她的病痛主导了我们的生活，决定了我们买什么、吃什么、去哪里旅行。我总觉得一定能找到答案，解释为什么会这样。后来我没花太久就知道，要是有答案，它很可能来自一个既不起眼却又极具挑衅力的人：科学家克劳迪娅·米勒。

paragraphs.en: On a warm Texas afternoon, Miller and her affable husband, Bob, lead me through the San Antonio Botanical Garden. A monarch flits by. “I’ve noticed so many fewer butterflies, so many fewer birds, even the last couple of years,” Miller observes. Her raspy voice comes out so quietly that, at times, my recording device fails to pick it up. People are perpetually leaning in close or asking her to repeat herself. At 78, Miller typically uses a cane, but Bob gets the walker out of the car so she can cover more distance. She wears her silver hair in a low side ponytail, fixed in place with a scrunchie.
paragraphs.zh: 在一个温暖的德州午后，米勒和她那位和蔼可亲的丈夫鲍勃带我在圣安东尼奥植物园里穿行。一只帝王蝶飞过。米勒说：“我注意到现在蝴蝶少了太多，鸟也少了太多，甚至这几年尤其明显。”她的沙哑嗓音很轻，有时我的录音设备都来不及接住她的话。人们总是不停地凑近她，或者请她重复一遍。78 岁的米勒通常会用拐杖，但鲍勃会从车里拿出助行器，方便她走更长的距离。她把银色的头发扎在一侧低马尾上，用发绳固定。

paragraphs.en: The line between science and wellness has been blurred beyond recognition. WIRED is here to help.
paragraphs.zh: 科学与健康之间的边界已经模糊到几乎无法分辨。《WIRED》来帮你把这条线理清。

paragraphs.en: With her wide, thin-rimmed glasses, Miller disappears into the scenery, but she’s a particularly visible presence in her field. Now a professor emeritus at the University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio, Miller has held several federal appointments, chaired National Institutes of Health meetings, testified before Congress, consulted for the Environmental Protection Agency, authored dozens of papers, and worked with the Canadian, German, Japanese, and Swedish governments. In all this, she has tried to make sense of and raise awareness for chemical intolerance. One patient advocate I interviewed called her “Saint Claudia” for her commitment to overlooked and misunderstood patients. Kristina Baehr, an attorney who defends victims of toxic exposures, told me, “To have experts like Dr. Miller tell them you’re not crazy, this is very real, is very life-giving to people. She’s able to validate their experience with facts, with science.”
paragraphs.zh: 戴着她那副宽边细框眼镜，米勒在景色里仿佛融化了，但在她所在的领域却格外引人注目。米勒现为圣安东尼奥德克萨斯大学健康科学中心的名誉教授，曾担任过多个联邦职位，主持过国立卫生研究院的会议，在国会作过证词，为环境保护署提供咨询，发表过数十篇论文，并与加拿大、德国、日本和瑞典政府合作。她一直在努力理解并提高公众对化学耐受性的认识。我的一位受访患者倡导者称她为“圣克劳迪亚”，因为她始终致力于那些被忽视和误解的患者。辩护毒性暴露受害者的律师克里斯蒂娜·贝尔对我说：“让像米勒医生这样的专家告诉他们——你们不是在胡思乱想，这是真的，并且是实实在在的生命问题——这对当事人来说非常有力量。她能用事实、用科学来确认他们的经历。”

paragraphs.en: One such fact, Miller explains, is this: Over the past century, the United States has undergone a chemical revolution. “Fossil fuels, coal, oil, natural gas, their combustion products, and then their synthetic chemical derivatives are mostly new since World War II,” she says. “Plasticizers, forever chemicals, you name it: These are all foreign chemicals.” They’re everywhere you look, in homes and offices, parks and schools. They’re also, Miller believes, making people very sick.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒解释说，这里面有这样一个事实：过去一个世纪，美国经历了一场“化学革命”。“自二战以来，化石燃料、煤炭、石油、天然气及其燃烧产物，以及随后出现的合成化学衍生物，大都属于新事物，”她说。“可塑剂、永续性化学品，统统可以算进去：这些都是外源性化学物。”这些东西无处不在，无论在家里、办公室、公园还是学校都能看到。米勒认为，它们也正在让许多人变得极度生病。

paragraphs.en: In 1997, Miller proposed a career-defining theory of how people succumb to this condition. It came with a technical-sounding name, toxicant-induced loss of tolerance, and a convenient acronym, TILT. You can lose tolerance after one severe exposure, Miller says, or after a series of smaller exposures over time. In either case, a switch is flipped: Suddenly, people are triggered by even tiny amounts of everyday substances—cigarette smoke, antibiotics, gas from their stoves—that never bothered them before. These people become, in a word, TILT-ed. It’s not unlike developing an allergy, when the body labels a substance as dangerous and then reacts accordingly.
paragraphs.zh: 1997年，米勒提出了一个决定其职业生涯方向的理论，用来解释人们如何“屈服”于这种病症。它有一个听起来很专业的名字——毒物诱导的耐受性丧失（toxicant-induced loss of tolerance），以及一个很便于记忆的缩写：TILT。米勒说，人们可以在一次严重暴露后失去耐受性，也可以在长期的多次低剂量暴露后逐渐失去耐受性。无论哪种情况，都会有一个“开关”被打开：人们突然会被微小剂量的日常物质——以前完全无所谓的东西，如二手烟、抗生素、煤气灶排气——所触发。用一句话来形容，这些人就是“被TILT化”了。这与过敏的形成有点相似：身体把某种物质标记为危险，于是据此做出反应。

paragraphs.en: In 1999, Miller and her colleagues designed the Quick Environmental Exposure and Sensitivity Inventory, or QEESI (pronounced “queasy”), a survey to help doctors and researchers identify chemically intolerant patients. I’ve seen the QEESI cited in papers from 18 countries, but to date, most physicians still don’t know much about it. “It’s very frustrating to try to get these ideas across,” Miller says. The major problem is that, assuming TILT accurately describes the process of becoming chemically intolerant, we don’t know what biological changes occur inside the sensitized body, why so many symptoms crop up, or why one exposed person gets sick while others seem to walk away unscathed. But Miller thinks she’s closer than ever to an answer.
paragraphs.zh: 1999年，米勒和她的同事设计了《快速环境暴露与敏感性清单问卷》，简称QEESI（读作“queasy”），这是一个帮助医生和研究人员识别化学不耐受患者的问卷。我在来自18个国家的论文里见过QEESI的引用，但至今大多数医生仍然对它知之甚少。米勒说，“想把这些观点讲清楚非常令人沮丧。”主要问题在于，即便我们假设TILT能够准确描述化学不耐受形成的过程，我们也不知道在敏化后的身体内部会发生哪些生物学变化、为何会出现这么多症状，以及为何同样接触某种物质时，有些人会生病而另一些似乎却毫发无损。不过米勒认为自己比以前更接近答案了。

paragraphs.en: At the botanical garden, we approach an orchid exhibit. Sticky heat engulfs us as we enter. Orchids of varying shapes and colors fill the greenhouse, including one with spindly chartreuse petals. “What do you call this?” Miller asks. My plant-ID app comes up empty. So it goes, too, for chemically intolerant patients: The condition defies easy observation. “The world becomes like a torture chamber, and then nobody believes you,” Miller says. “That’s the worst part.” After falling ill, some people become hermits out of fear of exposure, abandoning their friends and family to live in remote areas. For others, nothing can keep them from spinning out of control. My mother knew someone who tried to escape her triggers by moving to the country in a trailer. Eventually, even that became unmanageable, and the woman shot herself in the head.
paragraphs.zh: 在植物园里，我们走向一个兰花展区。刚进去时，一股黏腻的热气瞬间包围了我们。温室里摆满了不同形状和颜色的兰花，其中有一株带有细长黄绿色花瓣的品种。米勒问道：“这叫什么？”我的植物识别应用却一无所获。化学不耐受患者也是如此：这种状态难以被轻易观察。“世界会变成一个酷刑室，而后来没人相信你，”米勒说，“那是最痛苦的部分。”在生病后，一些人出于害怕再次暴露而变得像隐士一样，因而远离朋友和家人，搬去偏远地区生活。另一些人则无论如何也无法阻止自己失控。我的母亲认识一位女士，她试图通过住进拖车并搬到乡下来逃避自己的诱发源。最终，那也变得无法控制，最后那位女士开枪自尽了。

paragraphs.en: Imagine feeling incredibly sick every time you encounter a cloud of cologne or fresh paint, then being told you’re making it up. I thought about my mom. Sometimes, catering to her needs could feel exhausting. But what must that have been like for her? The thing was, I never doubted her condition—especially after what happened on one bad day.
paragraphs.zh: 想象一下，每次闻到一阵古龙水或新油漆的味道就严重不适，随后却被人告知你只是在胡编。那时我想到了妈妈。有时，照顾她的需求会让我感到很累。但对她来说，那种体验会是怎样的？问题在于，我从未怀疑过她的病情——尤其是在某个糟糕的日子里发生的事情之后。

paragraphs.en: It’s time to tell you the worst thing I have ever done to another person. When I was 10, my parents and I attended a family reunion. The trip was difficult. We fought nonstop. Everyone cried. The photographer hired to capture a family portrait accidentally exposed the film to light, and it was just as well. If we could have wiped the whole week from our minds, we would have.
paragraphs.zh: 是时候告诉你我对别人做过的最坏的一件事了。我10岁那年，父母和我一起参加了一次家庭聚会。这趟旅程很艰难。我们持续不断地争吵，大家都哭了。受聘来拍家庭合影的摄影师不小心把底片暴露在光线下，结果恰到好处。要是我们能把那一整周从脑海里抹掉就好了。

paragraphs.en: During that ill-fated trip, my aunt gifted me a set of scented lip balms. My mom offered her a tight smile but, once we were alone, told me to toss them out. Instead, I hid them and, soon, weaponized them. After yet another argument, I sneaked into my mom’s room, peeled back her pillow case, and smeared the lip balms directly onto her pillow. Later that night, as she tried to sleep, she kept waking up, sicker and sicker, her head pounding. Finally, her nose helped her uncover what I’d done. She found the telltale smudges. The next day, my dad told me how she’d sobbed and howled, “Why would she do this to me?”
paragraphs.zh: 在那次倒霉旅行中，我的姑姑送了我一套有香味的润唇膏。妈妈对她报以僵硬的笑容，等我们单独在一起后，她让我把它们扔掉。可我没有这么做，而是把它们藏起来，很快又把它们拿来“武器化”了。又一次争吵后，我溜进妈妈的房间，掀开她的枕套，把润唇膏直接抹在她的枕头上。那天晚上，当她想入睡时，反复醒来，越来越难受，头也越来越痛。最后，是她的鼻子帮她发现了我做了什么。她找到了明显的污迹。第二天，爸爸告诉我，她当时又哭又叫地喊：“她为什么要这样对我？”

paragraphs.en: It remains, for me, a source of immense guilt. Later, I realized it was also the crumb of proof I needed. This was not all in my mother’s head.
paragraphs.zh: 对我来说，这件事一直是巨大的负罪来源。后来我意识到，这也是我后来需要的一点证据。我的母亲并不是“神经过敏”——这完全不是她脑子里的胡思乱想。

paragraphs.en: I’m at Miller’s condo across the street from the UT Health Science Center, with Miller and two of her collaborators. Her dining room table is lined with household products—a votive candle, a box of matches, body lotion, scented dish detergent. Beside them are chunky gadgets that look like something out of Ghostbusters. These are particulate monitors, which measure down to parts per billion. They need to be hypersensitive, because products like the ones on the table expel tiny molecules, and people with chemical intolerance seem to react to even minuscule doses. It’s akin to someone having an allergic reaction to a bag of peanuts opened on the other end of the airplane. One of her collaborators strikes a single match in front of a sensor. The number on the screen rockets from 0 to almost 500,000 parts per billion. It’s not always about what the nose can detect—though, in this case, sulfur dioxide fills the room.
paragraphs.zh: 我现在在Miller的公寓里，她住在得州大学健康科学中心（UT Health Science Center）对面的街道上，和Miller以及她的两位合作者一起。她的餐桌上摆满了日用品——一支油灯蜡烛、一盒火柴、身体乳、香味洗洁精。它们旁边放着一些笨重的仪器，看起来像《捉鬼敢死队》里的东西。它们是颗粒物监测仪，灵敏度可以精确到十亿分之一。它们必须非常敏感，因为桌上的这些产品会释放出微小分子，而患有化学物质不耐受的人似乎会对极其微小的剂量也产生反应。这就像有人在飞机另一端打开一袋花生，却让另一个人立刻过敏。她的一位合作者在传感器前点燃了一根火柴。屏幕上的数字从0飙升到接近50万，单位是每十亿分之一。问题不总是由鼻子能闻到的气味来决定——尽管在这种情况下，室内充满了二氧化硫。

paragraphs.en: Last year, Miller’s team published research on house calls for nearly 40 people with chemical intolerance. They measured indoor pollution from products like these, as well as other irritants like dust and mold, and performed blood tests for allergies. Then they recommended tossing out scented candles or moving cans of gasoline from an attached garage to a separate storage unit, and gifted the subjects natural cleaning products. They retested the homes several more times over the course of the year. As the indoor pollution decreased, the subjects’ symptoms improved.
paragraphs.zh: 去年，米勒的团队对近40名患有化学不耐受的人进行了入户走访研究。他们检测了这些产品产生的室内污染物，以及尘埃、霉菌等其他刺激物，并进行了过敏血液检测。随后，他们建议把香薰蜡烛扔掉，或将附属车库里的汽油罐搬到单独的储藏单元，并给受试者提供了天然清洁用品。随后一年内又对这些家庭进行了多次复查。随着室内污染水平下降，受试者的症状也随之改善。

paragraphs.en: For years, research like this convinced Miller that there was, indeed, something very wrong with her patients. But, again, that pesky mechanism for disease eluded her. Then she learned more about mast cells.
paragraphs.zh: 多年来，这类研究让米勒确信，她的患者确实存在问题。但同样地，引起疾病的关键机制始终难以捉摸。后来，她对肥大细胞有了更深入的了解。

paragraphs.en: Mast cells are a type of white blood cell that exists in nearly every tissue, including the skin, airways, and gastro-intestinal tract. When they detect something harmful, they can release hundreds of mediators, including histamine, substances that create symptoms like hives or swelling during anaphylactic shock. If the cells become overreactive, releasing too many mediators at the wrong time, a person can end up flushed, dizzy, wheezy, or exhausted. This is called mast cell activation syndrome, or MCAS. When Miller came across a book on the subject by Lawrence Afrin, a hematologist and mast cell disease researcher based in New York, she thought it sounded a lot like chemical intolerance. She called him. Many of his MCAS patients, it turned out, were sensitive to fragrances and medications.
paragraphs.zh: 肥大细胞是几乎存在于所有组织中的一种白细胞，包括皮肤、气道和胃肠道。它们检测到有害物质时，可以释放数百种介质，包括组胺，这些物质会在过敏性休克中引发荨麻疹或肿胀等症状。如果这些细胞反应过度，在错误的时间释放过量介质，人体可能会出现潮红、头晕、喘鸣或疲惫。这被称为肥大细胞激活综合征，即 MCAS。当米勒接触到一位研究该领域的作家劳伦斯·阿夫林（Lawrence Afrin）的一本书时——他是纽约的一位血液科医生，也是肥大细胞疾病研究者——她觉得这与化学不耐受非常相似。于是她给他打了电话。结果发现，他的许多 MCAS 患者对香味和药物都很敏感。

paragraphs.en: In 2021, Miller published what she considers her second eureka moment: a paper, coauthored with Afrin and others, that explains a potential link between TILT and MCAS. The team surveyed MCAS patients and found that those who scored high on MCAS questionnaires scored high on the QEESI. These patient groups also had nearly identical symptom patterns. Had Miller’s patients had mast cell activation syndrome this whole time? MCAS is a tricky disease to diagnose and treat, but it was something. An answer for the scientific community. An answer for her patients. And an answer for herself.
paragraphs.zh: 在2021年，米勒发表了她认为的第二次“灵光一现”时刻：一篇与阿夫林等人共同撰写的论文，提出了 TILT 与 MCAS 之间可能关联的解释。研究团队对 MCAS 患者进行了调查，发现 QEESI 得分高的人在 MCAS 量表上的得分也很高。这些患者群体也几乎呈现出相同的症状模式。米勒的患者始终是否患有肥大细胞激活综合征？MCAS 的诊断和治疗都很棘手，但这至少给了一个答案。一个给科学界的答案。一个给患者的答案。也是一个给她自己的答案。

paragraphs.en: Because that’s the other part of this story, the part Miller hasn’t been comfortable talking about until now: She, the condition’s leading researcher, suffers from chemical intolerance too.
paragraphs.zh: 这正是这个故事的另一面，直到现在米勒才不太愿意谈起的那一部分：这位该病研究的主要学者本人也同样患有化学不耐受。

paragraphs.en: Miller has difficulty searching her memory about her past. Exact years don’t come back to her, her retellings wander. Attribute it to age or brain fog or both. Still, over hours of conversations and dozens of emails, her story came together.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒在回忆过去时会感到困难。准确的年份想不起来了，她的叙述也常常会跑题。可以把这一点归因为年龄增长，或是脑雾，或两者兼有。尽管如此，在持续了数小时的交谈和数十封邮件交流后，她的故事终于逐渐拼凑完整。

paragraphs.en: Born in Milwaukee as the only child of a patent attorney and a teacher, Miller was drawn to science from a young age. After earning her BA in molecular biology from the University of Wisconsin–Madison and her master’s in environmental health from UC Berkeley, she spent several years in Chicago working for OSHA and the United Steelworkers union as an industrial hygienist, touring steel mills, coke ovens, and smelters to monitor worker health and safety. She began seeing snapshots of the condition that would define her career. Once, she was called to meet with women who soldered in an electronics plant. “They had some outbreaks of so-called mass psychogenic illness,” Miller said. “A manager brought one of these women into the office. He actually started soldering right in front of us and she starts to have her symptoms, sneezing or whatever.” To the manager, this proved that it was psychological—why should his worker be impacted if he and Miller were not? Miller suspected something else was at play, though she couldn’t put her finger on what.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒出生于密尔沃基，是一位专利律师和一位教师的独生女，从小就对科学很有兴趣。她先后在威斯康星大学麦迪逊分校获得分子生物学学士学位，在加州大学伯克利分校获得环境健康硕士学位，随后在芝加哥为 OSHA 和美国钢铁工人工会工作了数年，担任工业卫生师，巡查钢铁厂、高炉和冶炼厂，监测工人的健康与安全。她开始在工作中看到了一些后来定义她职业生涯的线索。一次，她被叫去与一群在电子厂焊接的女性见面。“那里的工人出现过所谓的群体心理性发病，”米勒说。“一位经理把其中一个女性带进办公室。他当场给我们示范了焊接，而那个女性马上就出现症状了：开始打喷嚏之类的。”对那位经理来说，这证明了问题是心理性的——如果他和米勒都没事，她的工人为什么会受影响呢？米勒却怀疑还有别的原因在起作用，尽管当时还说不清是什么。

paragraphs.en: She met Bob, a fellow industrial hygienist, through OSHA. By 1977, the Millers were newlyweds living in an old home in a verdant area of Lake Forest. They loved gardening and the foxes that visited the nearby pond. But the house had wasps and spiders. Miller did her research and found an EPA-approved pesticide for indoor use, which an exterminator sprayed on her floorboards and eaves. “That changed our whole lives,” Miller said.
paragraphs.zh: 她在 OSHA 通过 Bob 认识了他——也是一名工业卫生师。到 1977 年，米勒夫妇刚刚步入婚姻生活，住在 Lake Forest 一处环境清新的旧房子里。他们热爱园艺，也喜欢那片湖边池塘常来串门的狐狸们。可那栋房子里却有黄蜂和蜘蛛。米勒做了调查，找到了美国环保署批准的室内使用杀虫剂，让灭虫公司把它喷洒在地板板条和屋檐上。“那彻底改变了我们的整个人生，”米勒说。

paragraphs.en: Immediately, Miller was walloped with fatigue and mired in confusion. Her husband felt OK, so they decided to still go on their honeymoon in New Orleans. They left their two-month-old Burmese kitten with Miller’s parents. On the trip, they got a call. “This cat looks kind of droopy,” Miller remembers her parents saying. The next day, the kitten died.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒很快就被一阵强烈的疲劳压垮，心里一片困惑。丈夫身体状况还算正常，于是他们仍决定去新奥尔良度蜜月。她们把两个月大的缅因猫幼崽（暹罗猫）留在米勒的父母家照看。旅行途中，她们接到一个电话。米勒记得父母说：『这只猫看起来有点无力下垂。』第二天，这只小猫就死了。

paragraphs.en: Miller suspected that the pesticide had affected both her and her cat, but she couldn’t figure out how to get well. Then she was referred to Theron Randolph, an infamous allergist who broke ranks with the medical establishment after working with chemically sensitive patients. Other allergists stood by the idea that “the dose makes the poison”—basically, that any substance, even water or oxygen, can be harmful in excess, but trace chemicals shouldn’t sicken patients. Randolph disagreed, saying that small doses mattered and that bodies could accumulate toxic burdens over time. He also mounted a campaign against corn, believing it caused inflammation and brain fog. For this and other work, he was ousted from his faculty position at Northwestern University Medical School. By the time Miller met him, he’d become a lightning rod for criticism from peers, who accused him of relying too heavily on patient testimonials and unconventional testing methods.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒怀疑这种杀虫剂影响了她和她的猫，但她不知道怎样才能恢复健康。随后她被转诊给塞隆·兰道夫（Theron Randolph），一位臭名昭著的过敏科医生，因与化学敏感患者合作而与医学界决裂。其他过敏科医生坚持“剂量决定毒性”这一观点——也就是任何物质，即使是水或氧气，过量也会有害，但痕量化学物不应该让患者生病。兰道夫持不同意见，他认为微量暴露也很关键，身体会随时间积累毒性负荷。他还发起了反对玉米（corn）的运动，认为玉米会引发炎症和脑雾。因为这类观点和其他做法，他被北西南大学医学院的教职从队伍中逐出。等到米勒见到他时，他早已成为同行批评的焦点，被指过度依赖患者证词并采用了非常规检测方法。

paragraphs.en: “The world becomes like a torture chamber, and then nobody believes you,” Miller says. “That’s the worst part.”
paragraphs.zh: “这个世界会变得像一个拷问室，而别人却不相信你，”米勒说。“这就是最痛苦的地方。”

paragraphs.en: Unaware of this controversy and desperate to regain her health, she checked herself in for three weeks at one of the “environmental medical units” Randolph had established in the wing of a hospital. In Randolph’s opinion, Miller had to clear out her body before she could determine what was triggering her illness. Miller was confined to a unit with three other sick women, all with different symptoms. The rooms were outfitted with materials that wouldn’t outgas—ceramic-lined floors and walls, metal furniture. The hospital filtered in fresh air, with air-locked entrances. No disinfectants or fragrances were allowed inside. The program began with a nearly weeklong fast. By her third day, Miller felt incredible: “Your head is clear, you can remember things.”
paragraphs.zh: 她并不知晓这场争议，却迫切想找回健康，于是入住了兰道夫在医院一翼设立的“环境医学科”之一，住了三周。兰道夫认为，在判断是什么在诱发她的病症之前，米勒必须先清理体内的有害负担。米勒被安置在一个病房单元里，与另外三位不同症状但都生病的女性同住。房间内都采用不释放挥发性物质的材料——瓷砖衬里的地板和墙面、金属家具。医院会引入新鲜空气，并设置气闸门。室内不允许使用消毒剂和香味剂。治疗计划以近一周的断食开始。第三天时，米勒感觉非常好：『头脑清楚了，能记起事情了。』

paragraphs.en: It piqued Miller’s scientific curiosity. Randolph spent a few minutes with patients each day, and Miller flooded him with questions. Eventually, she delayed his rounds so much that he asked if she wanted to come to his staff meetings. Soon, she became a collaborator of sorts and, in the summer of 1979, presented at an NIH meeting on mass psychogenic illness. She discussed case studies of patients who fell ill after specific chemical exposures. This wasn’t hysteria, she argued; there was cause and effect. Afterward, she said, attendees lined up at the microphone to challenge her—a glimpse at the pushback that would shadow her for years.
paragraphs.zh: 这引起了米勒的科学好奇心。伦道夫每天会和患者谈几分钟，米勒便不断向她发问。最终，她的来访几乎打乱了他的查房安排，结果他问她是否愿意来参加他的全体会议。很快，她便在某种程度上成为了合作者。1979 年夏天，她在美国国立卫生研究院的一次会议上就“群体性心因性疾病”做了报告。她讨论了在特定化学暴露后生病的患者个案。她坚持认为这不是“歇斯底里”；这里有明确的因果关系。会后，她说有很多与会者排着队到麦克风前向她挑战，这让她得到了一个预示多年后将持续困扰她的先声。

paragraphs.en: Randolph suggested Miller attend medical school. If she had any hope of breaking through to the establishment, she recalls him saying, “you’ve got to learn everything they know.” But there were a few problems. The stay at the environmental medical unit only temporarily improved her health. When she returned to her Chicago home, she became sick again. So she and her husband moved to Texas, where Miller became a medical student at UT. “If I had revealed my own intolerances, I would never have been accepted,” she told me. She pretended she was merely interested in allergy and immunology. All the while, she privately struggled. If a patient came in reeking of cigarette smoke, she might be sidelined with dizziness. By that point, most meals made her sick too. “Her main food was chocolate,” Bob jokes. Sometimes, she would fast before her exams to try to regain some of the clarity she felt in Randolph’s care.
paragraphs.zh: 伦道夫建议米勒去读医学院。如果她想有机会打入主流医学界，他回忆说，她必须“把他们知道的都学会”。但问题不少。环境医学小组的停留只让她的健康暂时好转。回到芝加哥的家后，她又再次生病。于是她和丈夫搬到德州，米勒在那里成为得克萨斯大学的医学生。“如果我当时透露自己的不耐受情况，根本不可能被录取，”她告诉我。她假装自己只是对过敏与免疫学感兴趣。与此同时，她却在私下苦苦挣扎。如果有患者满身烟味走进来，她会感到头晕，于是被暂时放到一边。到那时，大多数饭菜也会让她不舒服。“她主要吃巧克力，”鲍勃笑称。有时她会在考试前禁食，试图找回在伦道夫照护下那种清醒感。

paragraphs.en: After earning her degree, Miller began her “real” work in earnest. She was appointed to the National Advisory Committee on Occupational Safety and Health, where she met Nicholas Ashford, an environmental policy lawyer and MIT professor. When the state of New Jersey tapped Ashford to study chemical sensitivity, he tapped Miller as his coauthor. So began a career-long collaboration. They published their New Jersey report in 1989, followed by a seminal book, Chemical Exposure: Low Levels and High Stakes, in 1991.
paragraphs.zh: 拿到学位后，米勒开始认真投入她所谓的“真正工作”。她被任命到职业安全与健康国家咨询委员会，在那里认识了尼古拉斯·阿什福德，一位环境政策律师，也是麻省理工学院的教授。当新泽西州请阿什福德研究化学敏感症时，他又聘请米勒作为共同作者。于是，两人开始了贯穿职业生涯的合作。1989 年，他们发表了新泽西州报告，1991 年又推出了具有里程碑意义的著作《化学暴露：低水平与高风险》。

paragraphs.en: Miller confided in Ashford about her condition, but he advised her to continue keeping it a secret. “You don’t want to cloud the good science that you’re doing,” she recalls him telling her. She obliged. The Department of Veterans Affairs contracted her to study Gulf War veterans exposed to chemical weapons who came home and could no longer tolerate common smells like WD-40 or a girlfriend’s nail polish. She corresponded with congressman Bernie Sanders for years to try to get the government to build environmental medical units. She met with some of the 100 EPA workers who, after their department installed latex-backed carpet, complained about blurred vision and chest pain. She testified before the Food and Drug Administration about patients who’d received breast implants and suddenly couldn’t drink alcohol or caffeine. All the while, she suffered her illness in silence. Then, some 40-plus years after her pesticide exposure, Miller found her way to mast cells—and, with that, the confidence to finally come forward.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒把自己的病情向阿什福德倾诉，但他建议她继续保密。她记得他对她说：“你不希望影响你正在做的好科学。”她照做了。退伍军人事务部委托她研究回国后再也无法忍受 WD-40 或女友指甲油等常见气味的海湾战争老兵。她与伯尼·桑德斯议员通信多年，试图促使政府建立环境医学科室。她还会见了 100 名美国环保局员工中的几位，那些人在部门铺设乳胶底层地毯后出现视力模糊和胸痛。她在美国食品药品管理局前作证，讲述一些接受了乳房植入后突然无法饮酒或喝咖啡因饮料的病人。与此同时，她一直默默承受着自己的疾病。直到在农药接触后四十多年后，米勒找到了“肥大细胞”这个切入点，并由此找回了终于公开站出来的勇气。

paragraphs.en: When Miller and I are alone in her condo, she shows me some of her art. In her office, we stand before two illustrations depicting Don Quixote and his famously misguided quest to become a hero. The first piece shows the aftermath of when he mistook windmills for giants and attacked them. He and his horse lay on the ground, battered, with their legs in the air. “He’s tilting at windmills,” she says. “That’s what I feel like I’m doing.” I understand the play on words, but I don’t say what I’m thinking: that Don Quixote, in his deep obsession, imagined enemies where there were none.
paragraphs.zh: 当我和米勒独自在她的公寓时，她会给我看她的一些作品。在她的办公室里，我们站在两幅描绘《唐·吉诃德》及其著名而误入歧途的英雄梦的插图前。第一幅展示了他把风车误认为巨人并向其发起攻击后的场景。他和他的马倒在地上，衣衫褴褛，双腿朝天。她说：“他在斜刺里昂。” “我也有这种感觉。”我听出了她的双关语，但没有说出我心里的想法：在他深深的执念里，唐·吉诃德把不存在的敌人都想象成了现实。

paragraphs.en: Miller comes across as single—almost monolithically—minded. Her preferred talking points include Elon Musk, whom she brought up to me by phone, in person, over emails. Her research has found that those with high chemical-intolerance scores are 5.7 times more likely to have a child with autism and 2.1 times more likely to have one with ADHD. (Sample size of one here, but I, a child of a chemically intolerant woman, have ADHD.) During my visit, Miller handed me a copy of Musk’s mother’s memoir, which has one line mentioning that she painted her husband’s plane while pregnant with Elon. Miller speculates that this exposure may have influenced his neurological development. (In 2021, Musk publicly stated he has Asperger’s, an autism spectrum disorder.) An article about this “could crack open the field,” as she put it. Perhaps, she wondered aloud, he might even build Randolph-style environmental medical units. Unprompted, Miller wrote me an email one day that read, in its entirety: “When I was an eight-year-old girl, living in Milwaukee, I never imagined I would become a doctor and diagnose the richest man in the world.”
paragraphs.zh: 米勒给人的感觉是单一——近乎一体化——地关注自己认为重要的话题。她喜欢讨论的议题之一是埃隆·马斯克，她通过电话、当面交流和邮件向我反复提起他。她的研究发现，化学不耐受评分很高的人，其子女患自闭症的概率高出5.7倍，患多动症（ADHD）的概率高出2.1倍。（这里面有一个样本极小的问题：那次样本只有一个，但我自己就是一位化学不耐受母亲的孩子，也有ADHD。）在我去拜访时，米勒递给我一本马斯克母亲的回忆录，里面有一句提到她在怀孕时给埃隆的父亲的飞机画画。米勒推测，这种暴露可能影响了他的神经系统发育。（2021年，马斯克公开表示自己有亚斯伯格综合征，这是一种自闭谱系障碍。）她说过，这篇文章“可能会打开这个领域的大门”。她甚至大声猜想，也许他甚至会建立“兰道夫式”的环境医学诊疗单元。有一次她主动给我发了一封完整的一句话邮件：“我还是个八岁小女孩，住在密尔沃基时，从没想过自己将来会成为医生，给世界上最富有的人下诊断。”

paragraphs.en: Another sticking point: terminology. She reviles the name “multiple chemical sensitivity,” which she sees as a stigmatizing and imprecise label. On its face, the term does not acknowledge patients TILT-ed by, say, mold exposure, a common initiating event. It’s also dismissed in lawsuits under the Daubert and Frye standards, which let judges block expert testimony on conditions lacking wide scientific acceptance. Multiple chemical sensitivity may describe how many patients feel, Miller says, but it’s a diagnosis without a clear medical explanation. Chemical intolerance is a more accurate term, she argues, and TILT is that missing medical explanation—and that should be the focus of research. This has become one of the issues at the core of her fracturing with the chemical intolerance community. The other, no surprise, is money.
paragraphs.zh: 另一个卡壳点是术语。她痛斥“多重化学敏感”这个名称，认为这是一个带有污名化且不准确的标签。这个词面上并不承认患者会被某些暴露——比如霉菌暴露，一个常见的诱发因素——所“倾斜”影响。它在法律上也常在达伯特标准和弗莱标准下被法院排除，这些标准允许法官阻止对缺乏广泛科学共识的疾病发表专家证言。米勒说，多重化学敏感也许能描述很多患者的感受，但它是一个没有明确医学解释的“诊断”。相比之下，“化学不耐受”才是更准确的术语，她认为TILT就是那种缺失的医学解释，也应当成为研究重点。这个问题已经成为她与化学不耐受社群分歧的核心之一。另一个核心，则不令人惊讶——是钱。

paragraphs.en: Funding is scarce in this niche and polarizing area. At UT, Miller was able to piece together government grants for some of her work, but she also routinely invested her own money. In 2013, everything changed. An heiress named Marilyn Brachman Hoffman died, leaving more than $50 million to a foundation in her name. Hoffman was a fellow sufferer of chemical intolerance and, throughout her life, corresponded with a handful of scientists, including Miller. In her personal will and trust, Hoffman also gifted $5 million to Harvard for research on “toxicant-induced loss of tolerance.” She noted that Miller should join the advisory committee. Indeed, Miller did so for a year as a part-time senior scientist. Then, in 2015, she and her colleagues set up the Hoffman Program for Chemical Intolerance out of UT Health San Antonio, with funding from the foundation.
paragraphs.zh: 在这个小而两极分化的领域里，资金十分稀缺。在得州大学（UT），米勒能通过政府拨款拼凑出一部分经费，但她也经常用自己的钱。2013年，一切改变了。一位名叫玛丽琳·布拉赫曼·霍夫曼（Marilyn Brachman Hoffman）的女继承人去世，留下五千多万美元给了以她名字命名的基金会。霍夫曼本身也是化学不耐受的受害者，并且在一生中与几位科学家保持书信往来，其中包括米勒。她在个人遗嘱和信托中还向哈佛大学捐赠了五百万美元，用于“毒物诱导耐受性丧失”研究。她特别写明米勒应加入咨询委员会。果然，米勒随后作为兼职高级科学家在那里任职一年。然后在2015年，她和同事们在圣安东尼奥德州大学健康科学中心（UT Health San Antonio）内建立了霍夫曼化学不耐受计划，资金来自该基金会。

paragraphs.en: The Harvard group never produced any research specifically on TILT (though they did study indoor air pollution, among other things). And, in recent years, the foundation has turned its attention elsewhere—namely, to research about multiple chemical sensitivity. Miller has felt left out in the cold. Hoffman specifically mentioned TILT in her will, not multiple chemical sensitivity. The executor of Hoffman’s will, an estate lawyer who became president of the foundation, worked for a large law firm that had defended pesticide and petrochemical companies. Was the foundation funding multiple chemical sensitivity instead of TILT, Miller wondered, as a way to delegitimize patients? (When asked for comment, the foundation said it’s still open to funding projects related to TILT but added: “The fact that we do not solely use the term TILT, which is almost exclusively associated with Claudia Miller’s work, may be a problem for her, but it is not a conspiracy to hurt people.”)
paragraphs.zh: 哈佛该团体实际上从未就 TILT 做过专门研究（尽管他们确实研究过室内空气污染等其他课题）。而且近几年，这个基金会已将注意力转向了其他方向——也就是多重化学物质敏感性研究。米勒感到自己被冷落了。霍夫曼在遗嘱中明确提到的是 TILT，而不是多重化学物质敏感性。霍夫曼遗嘱的执行人——一位随后成为基金会会长的遗产律师——曾在一家大型律所工作过，而该律所为农药和石化公司提供过辩护。基金会究竟是因为某种动机转向资助多重化学物质敏感性而不是 TILT，进而把患者边缘化吗？米勒心里如此猜想。（基金会在被问及评论时表示，机构仍然愿意资助与 TILT 有关的项目，但补充说：“我们并不单独使用 TILT 这一术语，而该术语几乎完全与克劳迪娅·米勒的工作相关，这可能让她不舒服，但这并不是为了伤害患者的阴谋。”）

paragraphs.en: Miller’s distrust is, in many ways, understandable. Her work butts up against the interests of huge companies and powerful people; she has spent her career watching her patients get dismissed. In an email to her coauthor Ashford, she mentioned that members of the foundation board considered her difficult: “Yes I am difficult—I am precise about my science and will not tolerate any tampering with the truth or any attempts to derail my research.”
paragraphs.zh: 从很多方面看，米勒的不信任是可以理解的。她的工作直接触碰到巨大企业和有权势人群的利益；她一生都在目睹患者被轻易否定。她在给共同作者阿什福德的一封邮件中提到，基金会董事会成员认为她难以合作：“我确实很难相处——我对科学非常严谨，不会容忍任何篡改真相或任何试图搅乱我研究的做法。”

paragraphs.en: I witnessed a piece of the drama myself when I attended an international conference on chemical intolerance, held over Zoom. Though there was one talk specifically on TILT, most of the presenters used the term multiple chemical sensitivity. After a Canadian physician concluded one session, the hosts fielded an audience question. Miller’s coauthor, Ashford, crackled to life. He urged the conference attendees to read his work with Miller and Afrin on mast cells. “We think we have cracked the code on chemical sensitivity,” he said. He then pivoted to criticizing the conference. “Without clarifying what’s causing or priming the patient, we’re not going to get anywhere,” he said. “And I’m very disappointed to see this isn’t emphasized.” He blinked. Silence hung in the digital air. Finally, one of the cohosts diplomatically thanked Ashford for “that intervention.” (Later, Miller told me that she’d been invited to present but had refused because of the focus on multiple chemical sensitivity: “I just couldn’t stomach it.”)
paragraphs.zh: 我在自己参加的一次国际化学耐受性会议上也亲眼见到过这场博弈。这场会议通过 Zoom 举办。虽然有一场演讲是专门讨论 TILT 的，但大多数报告者都在使用“多重化学物质敏感性”这一说法。在一场环节中，一位加拿大医生发言结束后，主持人回应了听众提问。米勒的合著者阿什福德立刻“亮了起来”。他敦促参会者阅读他与米勒、阿弗林关于肥大细胞的研究。“我们认为自己已经破解了化学敏感性的密码，”他说。然后他转向对会议本身提出批评，“如果不先弄清是什么因素导致或使患者处于易感状态，我们就无从进展，”他说。“我非常失望，这一点没有被强调。”他停顿了下。数字空间里一阵沉默。最后，其中一位联合主持人很有礼貌地对阿什福德说“谢谢你那番补充”。（后来，米勒告诉我，她曾受邀发言，但因会议聚焦于多重化学物质敏感性而拒绝了。“我真的受不了。”）

paragraphs.en: Whether you consider it steadfastness or stubbornness or something else, Miller’s approach has come at the cost of her relationships. Many people I reached out to opted not to speak with me. I began to get similar reactions when I asked sources about her work. They didn’t want to criticize her: She has dedicated her life to this understudied condition, but …
paragraphs.zh: 不管你把它看作坚定、倔强，还是别的什么，米勒的做法都以牺牲她的人际关系为代价。许多我联系过的人都选择不与我交谈。当我向消息来源询问她的工作时，我也开始得到类似反应。他们并非不敢批评她：她把毕生精力都投入到这一研究不足的病症中，但……

paragraphs.en: Her biggest barrier is that TILT has yet to be proven. “Where the evidence is not strong, you very often find strongly held opinions,” Jonathan Samet, former dean of the Colorado School of Public Health and member of the Hoffman Foundation’s scientific advisory board, told me. When I asked him specifically about TILT, he took a deep breath. He noted that few people have been so serious about this issue as Miller. “I don’t want to go into a critique—I mean, I think it’s very reasonable to make hypotheses,” he said. “I think the more challenging question is: What is the research that actually tests the hypothesis?”
paragraphs.zh: 她最大的障碍在于，TILT 目前还没有被证明。科罗拉多大学公共卫生学院前院长、霍夫曼基金会科学咨询委员会成员乔纳森·萨梅特（Jonathan Samet）对我说：“当证据不充分时，你常常会看到人们抱有非常坚定的观点。”当我专门问到 TILT 时，他深吸了一口气。他指出，很少有人像米勒那样认真对待这个议题。“我不想去做批判——我的意思是，我认为提出假设是非常合理的，”他说，“真正更具挑战性的问题是：哪种研究真正检验了这个假设？”

paragraphs.en: Supposing that TILT is real, mast cells remain difficult territory. There’s no definitive cause of MCAS, only more (yes) hypotheses. Questions remain: Could TILT cause MCAS, or do patients have preexisting MCAS, which is exacerbated during exposure events? Are these conditions, in fact, related at all? “This is the danger of mistaking association for causation,” Afrin says. “Just because two things are associated does not even begin to say whether one causes the other.”
paragraphs.zh: 假设 TILT 确实存在，那么肥大细胞问题仍然是个棘手领域。到目前为止，MCAS 还没有确凿的病因，只有更多（是的）假设。问题仍在：TILT 是否会导致 MCAS，还是患者本来就有 MCAS，而在暴露事件中被加重？这些状况实际上是否有任何关系？“这就是把相关性误当因果性的危险，”阿弗林（Afrin）说，“仅仅因为两件事相关联，并不能开始说明其中一件导致另一件。”

paragraphs.en: That 2021 paper that Miller sees as the culmination of her life’s work? It was based on surveys of 147 diagnosed MCAS patients from Afrin’s clinic. Of that group, 59 percent—or 87 people—met the criteria for chemical intolerance. It’s intriguing data but by no means conclusive. Nobody has yet taken a cohort of TILT-ed patients and done lab testing to investigate whether they have MCAS. “In my opinion, that still needs to be done,” Afrin says. “Different doctors have different styles—and Claudia, she’s pretty convinced that we have enough associative evidence that it’s a slam-dunk case. But TILT is just one of a zillion different diseases that MCAS is capable of driving.” And who to fund that research? TILT-ed patients would need to be well enough to travel to a clinic, where MCAS testing then costs thousands of dollars.
paragraphs.zh: 那篇米勒认为是其毕生工作结晶的 2021 年论文，基于阿弗林诊所 147 名确诊 MCAS 患者的问卷调查。该组中，有 59%——也就是 87 人——符合化学不耐受的标准。这是很有意思的数据，但绝非定论。还没有人拿一组被确认为 TILT 的患者做实验室检测，以调查他们是否患有 MCAS。阿弗林说：“我认为这仍然需要去做。不同的医生有不同的做法——而 Claudia 相当确信我们已经有足够的关联性证据，这几乎是个十拿九稳的案例。但是 TILT 只是 MCAS 能导致的上千种疾病中的一种。”那么，谁来资助这项研究？患有 TILT 的患者必须足够健康才能前往诊所，而 MCAS 检测本身又要花数千美元。

paragraphs.en: Then there’s the string of bodies left in Miller’s wake: An investigator on her UT team stopped speaking to her after she disapproved of some of his research and sent him a cease-and-desist letter for using her survey methodology. Another former staff member, Tatjana Walker, is now executive director of the Hoffman Foundation. The relationship is respectful but strained. When I told Miller that I was arranging to meet Walker, who also lives in San Antonio, she insisted that I could simply call Walker instead. The next day, Miller sent an email to me, Walker, several members of the foundation’s scientific advisory board, and Ashford. In it, she tried to set up a meeting between Walker and me—at Miller’s condo.
paragraphs.zh: 然后是米勒身后留下的一连串“过世者”——也就是被连带受影响的人：她在德州大学团队中的一位研究员在她不认同他的一些研究后停止与她联系，并且她给他发了停止和终止合作的律师函，原因是他使用了她的调查方法。另一位前员工，塔特贾娜·沃克（Tatjana Walker），如今是霍夫曼基金会的执行主任。两人关系虽礼貌却紧张。我告诉米勒我正在安排与沃克见面，沃克也住在圣安东尼奥；米勒坚持说，我完全可以直接给沃克打电话。第二天，米勒给我、沃克、基金会科学咨询委员会几位成员以及 Ashford 发了一封邮件。邮件中，她试图安排我与沃克在米勒的公寓见面。

paragraphs.en: I arranged a separate chat with Walker over breakfast. Miller came up quickly. “She’s got a really strong vision of what she thinks the phenomenon is,” Walker said. “And I would not be at all surprised to find out that she’s correct.” As the saying goes, absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. “But, in science, we try to take a step back and look at the bigger landscape.” She added that the foundation funds work about multiple chemical sensitivity because that’s the most generally understood term. “Claudia’s invested a ton of time, a lot of thought, and, in many ways, her life,” she said. “That’s it: She’s not the only person who has a thought about it.”
paragraphs.zh: 我和沃克约了个单独的早餐见面。米勒很快赶来。沃克说：“她对自己认为的这个现象有着非常清晰的判断。” “我一点也不意外会发现她是对的。”她补充道，正如那句老话所说，缺乏证据并不意味着不存在。她还说，基金会资助了“多重化学敏感”方向的研究，因为这是目前最被普遍理解的说法。“克劳迪亚投入了大量时间、很多思考，并且在很多方面把自己的生活都放进去了，”她说，“就是这样：并不只是她一个人有这个想法。”

paragraphs.en: In Miller’s second Don Quixote art piece, the protagonist lies on his deathbed, looking back on his life. Soldiers in the foreground, a windmill in the distance. At this point in the novel, Don Quixote has given up his fantasies. He advises his beloved niece to never marry a man like him.
paragraphs.zh: 在米勒的第二幅《唐吉诃德》作品中，主角躺在临终的病床上，回望自己的一生。前景里有士兵，远处有一座风车。此时的《堂吉诃德》里，主人公已经放弃了他的幻想。他劝他的心爱侄女永远不要嫁给像他这样的人。

paragraphs.en: The Don Quixote art in Miller’s office.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒的办公室里，《堂吉诃德》的画作。

paragraphs.en: I still hoped that Miller’s work might bring my own family answers. About 14 years ago, my mom moved to a coastal town in Mexico. I called her up to discuss what I’d learned. As it turned out, she’d become familiar with mast cells several decades before. And? “When I got blood work done, it didn’t show high levels of mast cell activation,” she said. She’d tried a few commonly prescribed mast cell stabilizers, just in case. They didn’t work. My heart sank. Maybe, she added, the tests have changed since then. I told her it could take multiple tests to pin down an MCAS diagnosis and that Afrin said that MCAS patients often need to experiment with a cocktail of meds to find a combination that works. “I would try it again,” my mom said, kindly.
paragraphs.zh: 我仍然希望米勒的研究能给我自己的家庭带来答案。大约十四年前，我妈妈搬到墨西哥的一个海滨小镇。我给她打电话，和她讨论我了解到的内容。结果发现，她在几十年前就已经对肥大细胞有所了解。而且？“我做过血液检查，没有显示出肥大细胞活化水平偏高。”她说。她也曾为了保险尝试过几种常见处方药——抗肥大细胞稳定剂。都没有效果。我的心一下沉了下去。也许，她又补充道，那个时候的检测项目已经发生了变化。我告诉她，确诊MCAS可能需要做多次检测，并且阿弗林说MCAS患者通常要试验一组药物的“鸡尾酒疗法”，才找到有效组合。“我会再试一次，”我妈妈善意地说。

paragraphs.en: Whatever the case, since moving to Mexico, her health has improved. This aligns with the prevailing treatment for those with chemical intolerance: Avoid your triggers. My mom suspects that living in a foggy part of the San Francisco Bay Area with a lot of mold might have contributed to being TILT-ed. Where she lives now, it’s dry, and many buildings are open-air. She still gets sick and can’t tolerate fragrances and certain foods. But she’s able to go for walks on the beach and run errands. She has more energy at 70 than she did throughout much of my childhood. She says stress reduction was one of the best things for her—and accepting that she might always feel adrift. “I had to stop freaking out,” she said. (Not surprisingly, stress can also exacerbate MCAS flare-ups.)
paragraphs.zh: 不管具体原因是什么，自从搬到墨西哥后，她的身体状况有所好转。这与目前对化学不耐受患者普遍建议的治疗方式一致：回避诱发因素。我的妈妈怀疑，自己当年住在旧金山湾区一个雾气重、霉菌很多的地方，可能促成了她被 TILT（化学诱发低温反应综合征）缠上。现在她住的地方很干燥，而且许多建筑是开放式的。她仍然会生病，也仍然无法耐受香水气味和某些食物，但她能去海滩散步、去跑一些杂事。到了 70 岁，她比我童年时期认识她时更有活力。她说，减压是对她帮助最大的事情之一——还有就是接纳自己可能始终会有种飘忽不定的感觉。“我必须停止恐慌，”她说。（并不奇怪，压力也会加重 MCAS 的发作。）

paragraphs.en: Today, chemical intolerance is an accepted medical diagnosis in Japan and a recognized disability in Canada. It’s unclear what a path forward might look like in the United States, though the Hoffman Foundation recently put out a request for proposals, which mentioned interest in expanding on mast cell theories and TILT. Miller is ready for everyone else to come around. “I’m not saying I deserve a Nobel Prize,” she tells me. “But it’s at that level.” Her husband chimes in: “Basically a new theory of disease. She thinks big, but the rest of the world doesn’t think that way.”
paragraphs.zh: 如今，化学不耐受已在日本被接受为一种医学诊断，在加拿大被列为一种法定残疾。美国未来会走向什么方向尚不清楚，不过霍夫曼基金会最近发布了征集提案通知，提到希望扩大对肥大细胞理论和 TILT 的研究。米勒做好了等待其他人逐步接受的准备。“我不是说我应该得诺贝尔奖，”她告诉我，“但它的水平就是那个级别。”她的丈夫接话说：“这基本上是一种新的疾病理论。她思考得很大格局，但世界上大多数人并不这样想。”

paragraphs.en: Miller’s symptoms have improved, which she attributes to stabilizing her mast cells with antihistamines and cromolyn. She also takes pre- and probiotics, plus pancreatic enzymes, to aid her digestion. Still, she can’t drive—she has neuropathy, which she believes stemmed from her pesticide exposure. At the end of our day at the botanical garden, her husband gets behind the wheel of their SUV. Miller rides in back beside a roaring air filter, which she says prevents her from getting sleepy from a buildup of fumes. But she’s exhausted anyway, and the noise drowns out her voice, which is thinner than ever. “The question is, how do you get any of this into medical training?” Miller asks. Her eyelids droop behind her glasses.
paragraphs.zh: 米勒的症状有所改善，她认为这得益于用抗组胺药和色甘酸钠（Cromolyn）稳定了肥大细胞。她还会服用益生元与益生菌，以及胰酶来帮助消化。尽管如此，她仍不能开车——她有周围神经病变，她认为这是自己接触农药造成的后果。我们在植物园结束一天活动时，她的丈夫坐上他们的 SUV 开车。米勒坐在后座，旁边是轰鸣的空气过滤器，她说这样可以防止汽车尾气积聚让她犯困。可她仍然很疲惫，车内的噪音盖过了她的声音，她的声音也比以往更单薄。“问题是，这些东西怎么能被纳入医学教育体系？”米勒问道。她的眼皮在眼镜后面开始下垂。

paragraphs.en: There may not be an answer in her lifetime; I hope there will be one in mine. Walker told me that, back when she was working with Miller, “one of Claudia’s favorite expressions was: Science advances one funeral at a time.” It’s much harder to let go of your own ideas than it is to pick up the thread of someone else’s. Though, who knows, maybe that’s what progress requires, like Don Quixote surrendering his illusions on his deathbed. It made me think back to a night in San Antonio when I went out to dinner with Miller and her husband. They paused by a tall water fountain in the restaurant’s courtyard. Miller stepped close and tossed a penny underhand to make a wish. The coin winked in the air and then fell to the ground. Miller wasn’t concerned: “Some lucky person will find it exactly perfect.”
paragraphs.zh: 也许她这辈子都不会有答案；我希望我这辈子能有。我采访过沃克时，她说在和米勒共事时期，“克劳迪娅最喜欢的一句话是：科学一边一边地借着一次又一次的葬礼前进。”放下自己的想法往往比接受别人的观点更难。虽然也许这正是进步所必需的，就像堂吉诃德在临终前放弃自己的幻想一样。这个想法让我想起在圣安东尼奥的一个晚上，我和米勒以及她丈夫一起去吃晚餐。我们在餐厅庭院里的一座高大喷泉前停下。米勒走近，抛出一枚五分钱硬币，想许个愿。硬币在空中划出一道弧光后落在地上。米勒对此毫不在意：“一定会有幸运的人捡到它，而且是刚刚好的。”

paragraphs.en: Let us know what you think about this article. Submit a letter to the editor at [email protected].
paragraphs.zh: 请告诉我们您对这篇文章的看法。请将来信提交给编辑部，邮箱为 [email protected]。
